symbols.name: n
symbols.def: number of matched sets

symbols.name: i
symbols.def: matched-set index

symbols.name: m_i
symbols.def: number of positions in matched set \(i\)

symbols.name: j
symbols.def: position index in matched set \(i\)

symbols.name: a
symbols.def: potential-treatment label

symbols.name: Y_{ij}(a)
symbols.def: potential outcome for position \(j\) in set \(i\) under treatment \(a\)

symbols.name: H_{F,n}
symbols.def: Fisher sharp null \(Y_{ij}(1)=Y_{ij}(0)\) for every \(i,j\)

symbols.name: \Gamma_i
symbols.def: Rosenbaum hidden-bias bound for set \(i\)

symbols.name: \alpha
symbols.def: conditional test level

symbols.name: \Omega_n
symbols.def: assignment vectors with one treated position per set

symbols.name: Z_i
symbols.def: treated position in matched set \(i\)

symbols.name: Z
symbols.def: vector of treated positions

symbols.name: z
symbols.def: realized vector of treated positions

symbols.name: \mathcal R_i
symbols.def: sample space for the observed outcome record in matched set \(i\)

symbols.name: R_i
symbols.def: observed outcome record for matched set \(i\)

symbols.name: r_i
symbols.def: realized observed outcome record in matched set \(i\)

symbols.name: r
symbols.def: full observed outcome record \(r=(r_1,\ldots,r_n)\)

symbols.name: F_{0i}
symbols.def: law of \(R_i\) under \(H_{F,n}\), which does not depend on the treated position

symbols.name: F_{1ij}
symbols.def: law of \(R_i\) under a specified causal potential-outcome alternative when position \(j\) is treated

symbols.name: \mathcal R_i^Q
symbols.def: common record set on which every selected alternative likelihood ratio is finite and positive

symbols.name: Q_i(j;r_i)
symbols.def: selected Bayes-factor density \(dF_{1ij}/dF_{0i}(r_i)\) on \(\mathcal R_i^Q\)

symbols.name: Q
symbols.def: collection of conditional causal Bayes-factor scores at \(r\)

symbols.name: \pi_i
symbols.def: specified uniform alternative assignment baseline in set \(i\)

symbols.name: P_{\mathrm{alt},n}
symbols.def: joint law of assignment and outcome record under the specified causal alternative

symbols.name: P
symbols.def: conditional assignment law given \(r\) under the sharp null

symbols.name: \mathcal M_{0,n}(\Gamma;r)
symbols.def: conditional Rosenbaum null assignment-law class

symbols.name: P_{1,n}^{Q}(\cdot\mid r)
symbols.def: posterior conditional assignment law under \(P_{\mathrm{alt},n}\) after conditioning on \(R=r\)

symbols.name: \phi
symbols.def: possibly randomized rejection rule after conditioning on \(r\)

symbols.name: \beta_n^\star(\alpha;Q,\Gamma,r)
symbols.def: conditional all-tests power frontier against the causal alternative

symbols.name: S_i
symbols.def: high-probability positions of a local Rosenbaum-polytope vertex

symbols.name: v_{i,S_i}
symbols.def: local extreme-tilt assignment vector indexed by \(S_i\)

symbols.name: P_v
symbols.def: global product law generated by local extreme tilts

symbols.name: \mathcal V_n
symbols.def: product extreme laws of the conditional Rosenbaum null polytope

symbols.name: \mathcal V_n^Q(r)
symbols.def: indexed terminal-block candidate vertices; under the additional strict score-ordering assumption, these terminal blocks are upper sets of the conditional Bayes-factor ordering

symbols.name: p_i^+
symbols.def: local classical tilt placing high probability on position \(m_i\)

symbols.name: P_n^+
symbols.def: product of the local classical extreme tilts

symbols.name: P_0
symbols.def: candidate simple null law in a Neyman--Pearson rule

symbols.name: c
symbols.def: likelihood-ratio cutoff

symbols.name: \rho
symbols.def: assignment-dependent rejection probability on a likelihood-ratio tie set

symbols.name: \lambda
symbols.def: mixing weights over global Rosenbaum vertices; support may optionally be restricted to \(\mathcal V_n^Q(r)\)

symbols.name: P_\lambda
symbols.def: convex mixture of global vertex laws; unless degenerate, it need not be conditionally independent or belong to \(\mathcal M_{0,n}(\Gamma;r)\)

symbols.name: \mathcal A_n(Q,\Gamma;r)
symbols.def: levels at which at least one generalized Neyman--Pearson rule calibrated under the classical tilt is valid for the full conditional Rosenbaum null

assumptions.condition: The Fisher sharp null holds: \(Y_{ij}(1)=Y_{ij}(0)\) for every \(i\) and \(j\).
Conditional on \(r\) under the sharp null, \(Z_1,\ldots,Z_n\) are independent.

novel.justification: This is an explicit alternative-model premise, not a consequence of the Rosenbaum null. It describes a prospectively randomized causal alternative in which treatment is assigned uniformly within independently randomized matched sets before the hidden-bias sensitivity model is imposed for the null comparison.

assumptions.condition: Under \(P_{\mathrm{alt},n}\), \(\Pr(R\in dr\mid Z=z)=\prod_{i=1}^nF_{1i z_i}(dr_i)\) for every \(z\in\Omega_n\).

novel.justification: This is an explicit alternative-model premise, not a property of pair-matched sensitivity-value theory. It is realistic for independently recruited matched sets with no cross-set interference or shared post-match shocks; for example, separately sampled matched strata with unit-level outcomes generated after one within-set treatment assignment.

assumptions.condition: For every \(i,j\), \(F_{1ij}\) and \(F_{0i}\) are equivalent measures, and \(dF_{1ij}/dF_{0i}\) has a finite strictly positive \(F_{0i}\)-almost-everywhere measurable version.
For every \(i\), the conditioning record satisfies \(r_i\in\mathcal R_i^Q\).
For every \(i\), the causal Bayes factors obey \(0<Q_i(1;r_i)<\cdots<Q_i(m_i;r_i)\).

novel.justification: The condition holds when a prespecified ordered causal mechanism makes the observed outcome record progressively more likely as the treated position moves up an ordered within-set response contrast. For example, it is satisfied by monotone exponential-family outcome alternatives with a strictly ordered sufficient-statistic contrast.

This is a finite conditional signal-strength condition: the causal outcome evidence distinguishing the two extreme treated positions exceeds the allowed hidden-assignment odds. It is met in ordered binary or exponential-family alternatives once the treatment-induced log-likelihood contrast is larger than \(\log\Gamma_i\).

assumptions.condition: Fix an integer \(m\geq2\), a conditioning record \(r_0\), a common \(\Gamma\geq1\), and a score vector \(Q=(Q(1),\ldots,Q(m))\in(0,\infty)^m\) whose endpoint labels are extremal, \(Q(1)=\min_jQ(j)<\max_jQ(j)=Q(m)\), and require for every \(n\) that the matched sets are conditionally independent replicas with \(m_i=m\), \(r_i=r_0\), \(\Gamma_i=\Gamma\), conditional alternative \(q^{\otimes n}\), where \(q_j=Q(j)/\sum_{k=1}^mQ(k)\), and conditional sharp-null class exactly \(\{\bigotimes_{i=1}^np_i:p_i\in\mathcal P_m(\Gamma)\text{ for every }i\}\).

novel.justification: novel: this is an explicit fixed-record, fixed-score conditional oracle experiment used to define the repeated-design benchmark; it is not the theorem's conclusion.

definitions.name: global conditional Rosenbaum extreme laws
definitions.construction: v_{i,S}(j)=\Gamma_i/(|S|\Gamma_i+m_i-|S|)\text{ if }j\in S\text{ and }v_{i,S}(j)=1/(|S|\Gamma_i+m_i-|S|)\text{ if }j\notin S;\quad\mathcal V_n=\{P_v:P_v(z)=\prod_{i=1}^nv_{i,S_i}(z_i),\ \varnothing\subsetneq S_i\subsetneq\{1,\ldots,m_i\}\text{ for every }i\}

definitions.name: conditional indexed upper-set-candidate extreme laws
definitions.construction: \(\mathcal V_n^Q(r)=\{P_v\in\mathcal V_n:S_i=\{m_i-k_i+1,\ldots,m_i\}\text{ for some }k_i\in\{1,\ldots,m_i-1\}\text{ for every }i\}\)

definitions.name: generalized conditional Neyman--Pearson rule
definitions.construction: \phi_{\alpha,P_0}^{\mathrm{NP}}(z;r)=\mathbf 1\{P_{1,n}^Q(z\mid r)>cP_0(z)\}+\rho(z)\mathbf 1\{P_{1,n}^Q(z\mid r)=cP_0(z)\},\quad c\geq0,\quad\rho\in[0,1]^{\Omega_n},\quad\mathbb E_{P_0}[\phi_{\alpha,P_0}^{\mathrm{NP}}(Z;r)]=\alpha;\text{ the vector }\rho\text{ is assignment-dependent on the tie set and is selected jointly with }c\text{ to meet every additional composite-null feasibility and complementary-slackness condition imposed by the testing problem}

definitions.name: least-favorable-mixture finite dual
definitions.construction: \min_{c\geq0,\,\lambda\in\Delta(\mathcal V_n)}\left\{c\alpha+\sum_{z\in\Omega_n}[P_{1,n}^{Q}(z\mid r)-cP_\lambda(z)]_+\right\};\quad \phi(z)=\mathbf1\{P_{1,n}^{Q}(z\mid r)>cP_\lambda(z)\}+\rho(z)\mathbf1\{P_{1,n}^{Q}(z\mid r)=cP_\lambda(z)\},\text{ where the assignment-dependent tie vector is selected so that }\mathbb E_{P_\lambda}\phi=\alpha,\ \mathbb E_{P_v}\phi\leq\alpha\ (P_v\in\mathcal V_n),\text{ and }\lambda(P_v)(\alpha-\mathbb E_{P_v}\phi)=0\ (P_v\in\mathcal V_n)\text{ together with the coordinatewise complementary-slackness equations}

definitions.name: conditional Rosenbaum validity region
definitions.construction: \mathcal A_n(Q,\Gamma;r)=\{\alpha\in(0,1):\text{there exist }c\geq0\text{ and assignment-dependent }\rho\in[0,1]^{\Omega_n}\text{ such that }\mathbb E_{P_n^+}[\phi_{\alpha,P_n^+}^{\mathrm{NP}}(Z;r)]=\alpha\text{ and }\max_{P_v\in\mathcal V_n}\mathbb E_{P_v}[\phi_{\alpha,P_n^+}^{\mathrm{NP}}(Z;r)]\leq\alpha\}

definitions.name: replicated conditional all-tests frontier
definitions.construction: \beta_{n,\mathrm{rep}}^\star(\alpha;Q,\Gamma)=\sup_{\phi:\{1,\ldots,m\}^n\to[0,1]}\left\{\mathbb E_{q^{\otimes n}}[\phi(Z)]:\sup_{p_1,\ldots,p_n\in\mathcal P_m(\Gamma)}\mathbb E_{\otimes_{i=1}^np_i}[\phi(Z)]\leq\alpha\right\};\text{ this is the specialization of }{\rm def:frontier}\text{ at }r^{(n)}=(r_0,\ldots,r_0)\text{ under }{\rm ass:replicated-conditional-design},\text{ not a competing estimand}

statements.statement: Under \({\rm ass:conditional-independence}\) and \({\rm ass:rosenbaum-odds}\), every conditional test \(\phi\) satisfies \(\sup_{P\in\mathcal M_{0,n}(\Gamma;r)}\mathbb E_P[\phi(Z)]=\max_{P_v\in\mathcal V_n}\mathbb E_{P_v}[\phi(Z)]\).
statements.justification: This lemma converts the bounded-odds product null into the exact finite product-vertex constraints used by every finite primal, dual, validity, and numerical certificate.
statements.gap: Without the reduction, the all-tests frontier retains an infinite composite-null supremum and exact size certificates are not finitely auditable.
statements.consumer: Conditional sensitivity software can report a maximizing product vertex together with the rejection rule as an exact size certificate.

statements.statement: If \(m_i=2\) for every \(i\), then, under \({\rm ass:sharp-null}\), \({\rm ass:alternative-prior-factorization}\), \({\rm ass:alternative-record-factorization}\), \({\rm ass:likelihood-common-support}\), \({\rm ass:conditioning-support}\), and \({\rm ass:causal-likelihood-separation}\), \(P_n^+\in\mathcal M_{0,n}(\Gamma;r)\), \(\phi_{\alpha,P_n^+}^{\mathrm{NP}}(\cdot;r)\) is level \(\alpha\) for \(\mathcal M_{0,n}(\Gamma;r)\), and \(\beta_n^\star(\alpha;Q,\Gamma,r)=\mathbb E_{P_{1,n}^{Q}(\cdot\mid r)}[\phi_{\alpha,P_n^+}^{\mathrm{NP}}(Z;r)]\) for every \(\alpha\in(0,1)\).
statements.justification: For pairs, endpoint separation and positivity imply the strict score order needed by the likelihood-ratio argument, so a separate likelihood-order premise is redundant. The classical tilt then dominates every product null for the coordinatewise increasing Neyman--Pearson rule.
statements.gap: This is the pair specialization of the conditional all-tests frontier. It does not assert a generic Huber--Strassen representative-pair theorem beyond the directly proved Rosenbaum product argument.
statements.consumer: Pair-matched analyses with a prespecified causal alternative may compute the exact conditional likelihood-ratio sensitivity test at every level.

statements.statement: Suppose \(\max_i m_i\ge3\). Derive necessary-and-sufficient inequalities in \((Q,\Gamma,\alpha,r)\) for the existence of \(\lambda\in\Delta(\mathcal V_n^Q(r))\) such that \(\phi_{\alpha,P_{\lambda}}^{\mathrm{NP}}(\cdot;r)\) is level \(\alpha\) for \(\mathcal M_{0,n}(\Gamma;r)\) and \(\beta_n^\star(\alpha;Q,\Gamma,r)=\mathbb E_{P_{1,n}^{Q}(\cdot\mid r)}[\phi_{\alpha,P_{\lambda}}^{\mathrm{NP}}(Z;r)]\). Equivalently, characterize \(\mathcal A_n(Q,\Gamma;r)\), where the mixture collapses to \(P_n^+\), and determine its level-dependent support changes outside that region.
statements.justification: The unresolved object is a conditional saddle-point family indexed by level, not a tail bound for a preselected statistic.
statements.gap: Rosenbaum1988SensitivityMultipleControls proves sharp sensitivity analysis for a selected generalized signed-rank family with arbitrary responses and multiple controls. CohenOlsonFogarty2020AdversarialGame optimizes a coherent linear class of prespecified one-sided statistics. Neither ranges over all finite conditional tests or identifies the least-favorable mixture; GastwirthKriegerRosenbaum2000Separability and Fogarty2025Tilted likewise retain a selected statistic.
statements.consumer: A level-indexed mixture supplies exact conditional calibration primitives for matched-set sensitivity software such as \(\texttt{sensitivitymv}\) and \(\texttt{senfm}\).

statements.statement: Suppose \(m_i=2\) for every \(i\), and suppose \({\rm ass:sharp-null}\), \({\rm ass:conditional-independence}\), \({\rm ass:rosenbaum-odds}\), \({\rm ass:alternative-prior-factorization}\), \({\rm ass:alternative-record-factorization}\), \({\rm ass:likelihood-common-support}\), \({\rm ass:conditioning-support}\), and \({\rm ass:causal-likelihood-separation}\) hold. Then \(\mathcal V_n^Q(r)=\{P_n^+\}\) and \(\mathcal A_n(Q,\Gamma;r)=(0,1)\).
statements.justification: The pair specialization collapses the indexed terminal-block family to the single classical tilt and makes that tilt minimax sharp at every nondegenerate level.
statements.gap: The dependency list records the full causal and conditional testing setup. A separate score-order premise is unnecessary for pairs because positivity and endpoint separation imply \(Q_i(2;r_i)>Q_i(1;r_i)\).
statements.consumer: The proposition connects the general all-tests formulation to classical pair-matched sign and McNemar sensitivity calibration without introducing a second pair-specific frontier.

sampling_model.design: One treated position is selected in each matched set; conditional on the sharp-null outcome record, set assignments are independent.
sampling_model.units: Matched sets \(i=1,\ldots,n\) contain \(m_i\) positions and potential outcomes \(Y_{ij}(0),Y_{ij}(1)\).
sampling_model.treatment: \(Z_i\) identifies the treated position and determines the observed outcome record \(R_i\).
sampling_model.overlap: The \(\Gamma_i\)-odds restriction gives strictly positive conditional null assignment probabilities; equivalent alternative record laws give common likelihood-ratio support.
sampling_model.nuisance_estimation: None: \(\Gamma\), the uniform alternative assignment baseline, and the alternative record laws are fixed inputs to the finite conditional game.

bibliography.citation: Rosenbaum, P. R. (1987). Sensitivity analysis for certain permutation inferences in matched observational studies. \emph{Biometrika}, 74(1), 13--26.
Rosenbaum, P. R. (1988). Sensitivity analysis for matching with multiple controls. \emph{Biometrika}, 75(3), 577--581. doi:10.1093/biomet/75.3.577.
Huber, P. J. and Strassen, V. (1973). Minimax tests and the Neyman--Pearson lemma for capacities. \emph{The Annals of Statistics}, 1(2), 251--263. doi:10.1214/aos/1176342363.
Gastwirth, J. L., Krieger, A. M., and Rosenbaum, P. R. (2000). Asymptotic separability in sensitivity analysis. \emph{Journal of the Royal Statistical Society: Series B}, 62(3), 545--555.
Zhao, Q. (2019). On sensitivity value of pair-matched observational studies. \emph{Journal of the American Statistical Association}, 114(526), 713--722.

Cohen, P. L., Olson, M. A., and Fogarty, C. B. (2020). Multivariate one-sided testing in matched observational studies as an adversarial game. \emph{Biometrika}, 107(4), 809--825. doi:10.1093/biomet/asaa024.

Fogarty, C. B. and Small, D. S. (2016). Sensitivity analysis for multiple comparisons in matched observational studies through quadratically constrained linear programming. \emph{Journal of the American Statistical Association}, 111(516), 1820--1830. doi:10.1080/01621459.2015.1120675.
Fogarty, C. B. (2025). Tilted sensitivity analysis in matched observational studies. \emph{arXiv:2503.09736}.

tldr: Conditioning on the observed records turns the Rosenbaum sharp-null problem into an exact finite all-tests linear program. The Rosenbaum-specific finite contribution is the product-vertex program, the indexed-support criterion \(D_{\mathcal B}=D_{\mathcal V}\), the exact classical-validity topology, and auditable support-switch certificates. In the three-position example, \(\mathcal A_1=(0,2/3]\); at level \(7/10\), the optimal rule is \((1/10,7/10,1)\), its power is \(197/260\), and the least-favorable weights are \(16/21\) and \(5/21\). A separate \(84\)-vertex diagnostic proves that coordinatewise monotonicity can cost exactly \(1/720\). In the fixed-record, fixed-score replicated oracle experiment, with positions \(1\) and \(m\) labeling the score minimum and maximum, the all-randomized-tests design-sensitivity boundary is \(\widetilde\Gamma_{\mathrm{all}}^\star(Q)=Q(m)/Q(1)\). Below it, the explicit endpoint score \(s_\Gamma(j)=\mathbf1\{j=m\}-\Gamma\mathbf1\{j=1\}\) yields uniform null size and alternative-error bounds \(e^{-c_0n}\), where \(c_0=\Delta^2/[2(\Gamma+1)^2]\); at and above it, the frontier equals \(\alpha\) for every \(n\). For \(m=2\), the boundary is \(Q(2)/Q(1)=q_2/(1-q_2)\), the sign/McNemar success odds.

project_justification.gap: Generic finite minimax theory does not expose the Rosenbaum product vertices, decide when a prescribed indexed terminal-block support is sufficient, identify the levels where the classical tilt is minimax sharp, or provide exact support-switch certificates. Existing asymptotic sensitivity results study selected procedures or sensitivity-value estimators rather than the maximal common-\(\Gamma\) detectability boundary over every randomized test.
project_justification.niche: The note joins two sharply delimited objects: an exact conditional finite all-tests linear program for heterogeneous matched sets and an oracle repeated-design benchmark with fixed \(m\), fixed record, fixed endpoint-extremal \(Q\), common \(\Gamma\), and independent replicas. The causal target remains the Fisher sharp null; the finite frontier and replicated design-sensitivity boundary are testing functionals, not alternative definitions of the causal null.
project_justification.fill: Vertex reduction makes composite-null validity finitely auditable. Linear-program duality and complementary slackness give the least-favorable mixture, assignment-dependent likelihood-ratio ties, the support equality \(D_{\mathcal B}=D_{\mathcal V}\), the tie-block representation of \(\mathcal A_n\), and the affine support-switch envelope. In the replicated design, the explicit endpoint score \(s_\Gamma(j)=\mathbf1\{j=m\}-\Gamma\mathbf1\{j=1\}\) has null supremum zero and alternative mean \(\Delta=[Q(m)-\Gamma Q(1)]/\sum_kQ(k)\). Hoeffding's inequality then gives both errors at most \(e^{-c_0n}\), while \(q\in\mathcal P_m(\Gamma)\) at and above the boundary gives the exact all-tests converse.

related_work: Rosenbaum's multiple-control analysis gives sharp sensitivity calculations for a selected generalized signed-rank family \cite{Rosenbaum1988SensitivityMultipleControls}. Gastwirth--Krieger--Rosenbaum establish asymptotic separability for specified rank procedures with multiple controls \cite{GastwirthKriegerRosenbaum2000Separability}; their result concerns selected procedures, whereas the replicated theorem here is an oracle detectability boundary over all randomized tests. Zhao derives asymptotic normality for the pair-matched sensitivity value \cite{Zhao2019SensitivityValue}; by contrast, \(q_2/(1-q_2)\) here is a population all-tests boundary, not an estimator or a sampling distribution. Cohen--Olson--Fogarty study coherent combinations of prespecified outcome statistics \cite{CohenOlsonFogarty2020AdversarialGame}; the present benchmark ranges over all randomized tests but is oracle and is not a practical coherent-statistic construction. Fogarty studies limiting laws and design sensitivity for tilted candidate statistics \cite{Fogarty2025Tilted}; the present result instead identifies the maximal common-\(\Gamma\) all-tests boundary and makes no operational-domination claim. Fogarty--Small share hidden-assignment probabilities across multiple outcomes \cite{FogartySmall2016MultipleComparisons}, a different optimization from the present single-outcome conditional frontier. Huber--Strassen provide generic robust-testing background, but their representative-pair conclusion uses a capacity structure not established for this Rosenbaum product-law envelope \cite{HuberStrassen1973Minimax}. The contribution here is the Rosenbaum-specific finite program and certificates together with the explicit endpoint-score repeated-design boundary, its uniform Hoeffding achievability, and its exact all-tests converse.
interpretation: The causal target is the Fisher sharp null, whereas \(\beta_n^\star(\alpha;Q,\Gamma,r)\) is the conditional all-tests power frontier after fixing the record. Positive causal Bayes factors determine the conditional alternative assignment law, while bounded Rosenbaum odds determine the composite product-law null. A least-favorable mixture is a convexified dual certificate and generally is not itself an independent assignment law in \(\mathcal M_{0,n}(\Gamma;r)\). The indexed terminal-block family requires no likelihood-order assumption; it becomes a \(Q\)-upper-set family only when strict score ordering is separately imposed. In the replicated benchmark the separating score is explicit: \(s_\Gamma(j)=\mathbf1\{j=m\}-\Gamma\mathbf1\{j=1\}\). It is an oracle, \(Q\)-known endpoint score, not the classical tilt, a selected practical rank statistic, a coherent combination, or an estimator when \(Q\) is unknown.
technical_internal_limitation: The finite characterization is exact, but the product-vertex count can grow exponentially. Indexed terminal-block support is certified by \(D_{\mathcal B}=D_{\mathcal V}\), not guaranteed. The \(n=2\), sizes \((4,3)\), common-\(\Gamma=2\) diagnostic checks all \(84\) vertices and exact unrestricted and monotone duals, proving optima \(143/240\) and \(107/180\) and gap \(1/720\); it is an obstruction to an unsafe monotonicity restriction, not a separate field-level novelty claim. Endpoint-touching components such as \((0,2/3]\) are intersections of ambiently closed projections with \((0,1)\) and need not be closed in \(\mathbb R\). The replicated theorem uses the explicit endpoint score and gives the achievable constant \(c_0=\Delta^2/[2(\Gamma+1)^2]\); it does not claim the optimal Chernoff exponent.
honest_scope: The finite results are conditional on the sharp-null sensitivity model, the factorized uniform-prior causal alternative, common likelihood support, and the fixed observed record. They deliver the exact all-tests envelope, product-vertex validity certificates, indexed-support criterion, validity-region topology, affine support diagnostics, the three-position switch, and the exact monotonicity-loss diagnostic. The asymptotic result is only for fixed \(m\), fixed record, fixed endpoint-extremal \(Q\), common \(\Gamma\), and independent conditional replicas whose null factors may vary across sets. It does not optimize the exponent, handle heterogeneous or growing sets, estimate \(Q\), prove unconditional validity, construct a selected rank statistic or coherent combination, establish the proposed two-sided \(0.158\) claim, prove Bonferroni equivalence, or identify a statistic-choice optimum.
